startScreen.startScreenSubtitle: A 70-year-old man presents with progressive hearing loss and dizziness. Apply your knowledge of the auditory and vestibular systems to guide the diagnostic workup.

endScreens.endScreenSubtitle: You correctly applied special senses physiology including auditory pathways, vestibular function, and cranial nerve involvement to this clinical scenario.
You showed some knowledge but missed key concepts. Review the auditory pathway, vestibular apparatus, and taste innervation.
Review the fundamentals: photoreceptors, auditory ossicles, organ of Corti, vestibular apparatus, and cranial nerve pathways for special senses.

params.text:
Case Presentation

A 70-year-old man presents with progressive hearing loss in the right ear over 6 months, accompanied by tinnitus and episodic vertigo. He also reports a change in taste sensation on the right side of his tongue.

Examination findings:

  • Weber test: Lateralises to the LEFT ear
  • Rinne test: Air conduction > bone conduction bilaterally (positive)
  • Right-sided facial weakness (upper and lower face)
  • Diminished taste on the anterior two-thirds of the right tongue

Sound is transmitted from the tympanic membrane through three ossicles — malleus, incus, and stapes — to the oval window. The organ of Corti in the cochlea contains the hair cells that transduce sound into neural signals.

branchingQuestion.question: Weber lateralises to the left (away from the affected ear) and Rinne is positive bilaterally. What type of hearing loss does the right ear have?

alternatives.text: Sensorineural hearing loss — damage to the cochlea or auditory nerve (CN VIII) on the right side

feedback.title: Correct!
feedback.subtitle: In sensorineural hearing loss, Weber lateralises to the NORMAL ear (left) because the damaged cochlea/nerve on the right cannot perceive the bone-conducted sound as well. Rinne remains positive (AC > BC) in both ears because both air and bone conduction are reduced proportionally. This pattern points to cochlear or retrocochlear pathology.

alternatives.text: Conductive hearing loss — a problem with the ossicular chain on the right

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: In conductive hearing loss, Weber would lateralise to the AFFECTED ear (right), not away from it. Also, Rinne would be negative (BC > AC) in the affected ear. This patient has Weber lateralising to the left and positive Rinne bilaterally, indicating sensorineural hearing loss.

alternatives.text: Mixed hearing loss — both conductive and sensorineural components equally

feedback.title: Not the best answer
feedback.subtitle: The tuning fork tests clearly indicate a sensorineural pattern: Weber to the normal ear and positive Rinne bilaterally. Combined with facial nerve involvement and taste loss, this suggests a cerebellopontine angle lesion affecting CN VII and VIII — not a mixed peripheral problem.

params.text:
Localising the Lesion

The combination of findings points to a cerebellopontine angle (CPA) lesion, most likely an acoustic neuroma (vestibular schwannoma):

  • Hearing loss + tinnitus: Compression of the cochlear division of CN VIII
  • Vertigo: Involvement of the vestibular division — the semicircular canals detect rotational acceleration, while the utricle and saccule detect linear acceleration
  • Facial weakness: Compression of CN VII (facial nerve)
  • Loss of taste: The anterior two-thirds of the tongue is innervated by the facial nerve (CN VII) via the chorda tympani; the posterior one-third by the glossopharyngeal nerve (CN IX)

MRI with gadolinium is the gold standard for confirming the diagnosis.

branchingQuestion.question: The patient's vertigo is episodic and triggered by head movements. The vestibular apparatus has two functional components. Which correctly describes their functions?

alternatives.text: Semicircular canals detect rotational (angular) acceleration; utricle and saccule detect linear acceleration and head position

feedback.title: Correct!
feedback.subtitle: The three semicircular canals (horizontal, anterior, posterior) are oriented in three perpendicular planes and detect angular/rotational acceleration via endolymph flow deflecting the cupula. The otolith organs (utricle for horizontal, saccule for vertical) detect linear acceleration and static head tilt via otoconia on hair cells.

alternatives.text: Semicircular canals detect gravity and static position; utricle and saccule detect movement

feedback.title: Reversed!
feedback.subtitle: This is backwards. The otolith organs (utricle and saccule) detect gravity and linear acceleration using otoconia (calcium carbonate crystals). The semicircular canals detect rotational/angular acceleration using endolymph flow. Benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV) occurs when otoconia dislodge into a semicircular canal.

alternatives.text: The cochlea handles both hearing and balance — the vestibular apparatus is not involved

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: The cochlea is specifically for hearing (organ of Corti with hair cells for sound transduction). Balance is handled by the vestibular apparatus: semicircular canals (angular acceleration) and otolith organs — utricle and saccule (linear acceleration and gravity). Both share the membranous labyrinth but have distinct functions.

params.text:
Key Learning Points

  • Photoreceptors: Rods (dim light, peripheral vision) and cones (colour, fine detail/acuity)
  • Auditory pathway: Tympanic membrane → malleus → incus → stapes → oval window → cochlear fluid → organ of Corti hair cells
  • Hearing tests: Weber lateralises to normal ear in sensorineural loss, to affected ear in conductive loss; Rinne negative in conductive loss
  • Taste innervation: Anterior 2/3 tongue — facial nerve (CN VII); posterior 1/3 — glossopharyngeal (CN IX)
  • Vestibular system: Semicircular canals (rotational acceleration); utricle + saccule (linear acceleration and gravity)
  • Acoustic neuroma: CPA tumour causing sensorineural hearing loss, tinnitus, vertigo, and facial nerve compression
